title: Inherited IRAs and SECURE Act Rules
description: Apply the 10-year rule for inherited IRAs
topics: Apply the 10-year rule for inherited IRAs
Compare pre-SECURE and post-SECURE inheritance rules

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The SECURE Act (2019) dramatically changed inherited IRA rules, eliminating the "stretch IRA" for most beneficiaries.

sections.title: Pre-SECURE: Stretch IRA
sections.content: Before SECURE Act, any designated beneficiary could take RMDs over their own life expectancy, stretching distributions over decades. Example: 30-year-old inheriting IRA could stretch over 53+ years—tremendous tax-deferred growth.

sections.title: SECURE Act: The 10-Year Rule
sections.content: Most non-spouse beneficiaries must now completely distribute the inherited IRA within 10 years. No more "stretch" over life expectancy.

sections.title: Five Eligible Designated Beneficiaries
items: 1. Surviving spouse - can treat as own
2. Minor child of deceased - stretch until majority, then 10-year (owner's child only, not grandchild)
3. Disabled individual - IRS definition
4. Chronically ill individual - requires certification
5. Not more than 10 years younger - siblings, elderly parents

sections.title: 10-Year Rule Details
rows: Distribute by end of year 10 (any timing)
Annual RMDs required + distribute by year 10

sections.title: Exam Tip
sections.content: IRS clarified: If owner died AFTER RMD age, beneficiary must take annual RMDs AND deplete by year 10. If owner died BEFORE RMD age, no annual RMDs required—just empty by year 10.

sections.title: Surviving Spouse Options
rows: Roll to own or elect as own

sections.title: Inherited Roth IRAs
sections.content: Same 10-year rule applies. Key difference: Distributions are tax-free (if original 5-year rule met), no annual RMDs required during 10 years. Best strategy: Wait until year 10 to maximize tax-free growth.

sections.title: Trusts as Beneficiaries
sections.content: See-Through Trust requirements: Valid under state law, irrevocable (or becomes so at death), beneficiaries identifiable, documentation provided. Two types: Conduit trust (must distribute all RMDs) and Accumulation trust (can accumulate, often 10-year rule).

sections.title: Summary
items: SECURE Act: 10-year rule for most non-spouse beneficiaries
5 EDBs can stretch: spouse, minor child, disabled, chronically ill, ≤10 years younger
If owner died after RMD age: Annual RMDs + deplete by year 10
Spouse can: treat as own, remain beneficiary, or elect 10-year
Inherited Roth: 10-year rule but tax-free distributions

sections.title: SECURE Act Impact on Inherited IRAs

knowledgeCheck.question: Under the SECURE Act, which of the following beneficiaries would be ELIGIBLE to utilize the 'stretch' provision for an inherited IRA, assuming all requirements are met?
options: A 45-year-old sibling of the deceased IRA owner.
A 35-year-old child of the deceased IRA owner.
A chronically ill adult child of the deceased IRA owner.
A trust established for the benefit of the deceased IRA owner's grandchildren.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The SECURE Act generally eliminated the stretch provision for most beneficiaries, requiring full distribution within 10 years. However, it created exceptions for 'eligible designated beneficiaries', including the surviving spouse, a minor child of the deceased (until they reach the age of majority), a disabled individual, a chronically ill individual, or any individual who is not more than 10 years younger than the deceased.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the exceptions to the 10-year rule created by the SECURE Act. Who qualifies as an 'eligible designated beneficiary'?

reveal.question: Describe the distribution requirements for a non-eligible designated beneficiary inheriting an IRA after the SECURE Act.
reveal.answer: The entire inherited IRA must be distributed within 10 years of the IRA owner's death. While there are no required minimum distributions (RMDs) in years 1-9, the entire balance must be withdrawn by December 31st of the 10th year following the year of death.

rows.label: Distribution Period
values: Beneficiary's life expectancy
10 years

rows.label: RMDs Required?
values: Yes, annually based on beneficiary's life expectancy
No, but entire amount must be distributed by the end of the 10th year

rows.label: Beneficiary Eligibility
values: Any designated beneficiary
Limited to eligible designated beneficiaries (spouses, minor children, disabled or chronically ill individuals, or those not more than 10 years younger) for stretch; others subject to 10-year rule

rows.label: Tax Deferral Potential
values: Significant; distributions stretched over many years
Limited; entire account must be distributed within 10 years

title: Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA)
topics: Calculate tax savings from NUA treatment

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: NUA is a tax strategy that can convert ordinary income into long-term capital gains when distributing employer stock from a qualified plan.

sections.title: What Is NUA?
sections.content: Net Unrealized Appreciation = Current FMV - Cost Basis. The increase in value of employer stock while held inside a qualified plan. Normal 401(k) distributions: Taxed as ordinary income (up to 37%). NUA strategy: NUA portion taxed as long-term capital gains (up to 20%).

sections.title: NUA Requirements
items: Must be from qualified plan (401(k), profit-sharing, ESOP) - NOT from IRA
Must be lump-sum distribution (entire balance in one tax year)
Triggering events: Separation from service, death, disability, after age 59½
Must distribute in-kind (stock goes to taxable account as stock)

sections.title: 10% Penalty Treatment
sections.content: If under 59½: 10% penalty applies to cost basis only (distributed from plan). 10% penalty does NOT apply to NUA (taxed as LTCG at sale, no penalty).

sections.title: Partial NUA Strategy
sections.content: Can split: Take some employer stock as NUA (to taxable account), roll the rest to IRA. Benefits: Some liquidity, manage current-year tax, diversify some while preserving NUA.

sections.title: Summary
items: NUA = employer stock appreciation in plan; taxed at LTCG rate
Must be lump-sum distribution from qualified plan (not IRA)
Pay ordinary income on cost basis at distribution; LTCG on NUA at sale
No 10% penalty on NUA portion (even if under 59½)
Best when: Large NUA, low basis, high marginal rate, need access

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would MOST likely make the Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) strategy advantageous for a client?
options: The client's marginal tax rate is 12% and they anticipate it remaining at that level in retirement; the stock has appreciated significantly while in the plan.
The client's marginal tax rate is 37% currently, they anticipate it dropping to 24% in retirement, and the stock has experienced minimal appreciation while in the plan.
The client's marginal tax rate is 24% currently, they anticipate it rising to 32% in retirement, and the stock has appreciated significantly while in the plan.
The client's marginal tax rate is 37% currently, they anticipate it dropping to 24% in retirement, and the stock has appreciated significantly while in the plan.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The NUA strategy is most advantageous when the client's current ordinary income tax rate is significantly higher than the long-term capital gains rate they will pay on the NUA portion, and the stock has appreciated substantially. This allows them to convert a portion of their retirement savings from ordinary income to long-term capital gains rates. Option D provides both of these conditions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the tax rate implications and the amount of appreciation.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA)?
reveal.answer: NUA = Current Fair Market Value (FMV) of Employer Stock - Cost Basis of Employer Stock.

rows.label: Tax Rate on Appreciation
values: Long-Term Capital Gains Rate (up to 20% + 3.8% NIIT if applicable)
Ordinary Income Tax Rate (up to 37%)

rows.label: Tax Rate on Cost Basis
values: Ordinary Income Tax Rate (up to 37%)
Ordinary Income Tax Rate (up to 37%)

rows.label: Stock Location
values: Employer Stock Distributed In-Kind to Taxable Account
Assets Remain Inside the Qualified Plan/IRA

rows.label: Lump-Sum Required
values: Generally Requires a Lump-Sum Distribution
Distributions Can Be Taken Over Time

title: Retirement Plans and Divorce (QDROs)
topics: Compare division of different retirement account types
Apply special tax rules for divorce-related transfers
Identify common QDRO mistakes to avoid

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Retirement accounts are often the largest marital asset after a home. Proper division requires understanding QDROs and special tax rules.

sections.title: What Is a QDRO?
sections.content: Qualified Domestic Relations Order: A court order that divides a qualified retirement plan in a divorce or separation. Needed because ERISA-covered plans are protected from assignment. QDRO allows division to an alternate payee (spouse, former spouse, child, dependent).

sections.title: Plans That Need QDROs vs. Not
IRAs: Transfer Incident to Divorce
sections.content: IRAs don't need QDROs. Divided by "transfer incident to divorce" under IRC 408(d)(6). Just need divorce decree or separation agreement. Tax-free transfer between spouses.

sections.title: QDRO Requirements
items: Must Include: Names/addresses, plan name, dollar amount or percentage, payment timing
Cannot Require: Benefits not otherwise available, increased benefits, benefits allocated to another payee

sections.title: Special Tax Rule: QDRO Exception
sections.content: Distributions from QDRO to alternate payee: NO 10% early withdrawal penalty if taken directly from the plan. BUT if alternate payee rolls to IRA first, then withdraws, 10% penalty applies if under 59½.

sections.title: Planning Tip
sections.content: If alternate payee needs funds, take from plan BEFORE rolling to IRA. This preserves the penalty-free access. Once in IRA, penalty-free access is lost.

sections.title: Valuing Retirement Accounts
sections.content: Defined Contribution: Simple - account balance at division date. Defined Benefit: Complex - requires actuarial present value calculation or coverture fraction. Coverture Fraction = Years Married During Employment / Total Years of Employment.

sections.title: Coverture Example
sections.content: 25 years total employment, 15 years married. Pension = $3,000/month. Coverture = 15/25 = 60%. Marital portion = $1,800/month. If split 50/50: Alternate payee = $900/month.

sections.title: Common QDRO Mistakes
rows: Rolling to IRA before taking needed funds
May not work for specific plan

sections.title: Summary
items: QDRO required for qualified plans (401(k), 403(b), pension); not for IRAs
IRAs: Transfer incident to divorce = tax-free, no QDRO needed
QDRO distribution exception: No 10% penalty from plan (even under 59½)
Rolling to IRA first loses penalty-free access
Coverture fraction = marital years / total employment years

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements concerning Qualified Domestic Relations Orders (QDROs) is CORRECT?
options: A QDRO can be used to divide any type of retirement account, including IRAs and non-qualified deferred compensation plans.
A QDRO is required to divide a qualified retirement plan incident to a divorce, allowing the transfer to an alternate payee without triggering a taxable event or early withdrawal penalty.
The alternate payee in a QDRO is always the former spouse.
A QDRO is executed after the divorce decree is finalized, based on the division of assets outlined in the agreement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A QDRO is specifically designed for qualified retirement plans covered by ERISA. It avoids tax consequences and penalties when dividing these assets during a divorce. IRAs require a transfer incident to divorce under IRC Section 408(d)(6), not a QDRO. The alternate payee can be a spouse, former spouse, child, or other dependent. The QDRO process should ideally begin during the divorce proceedings to ensure timely execution.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which plans are governed by ERISA and the specific purpose of a QDRO.

reveal.question: What is the PRIMARY reason a QDRO is necessary for dividing qualified retirement plans in a divorce?
reveal.answer: ERISA (Employee Retirement Income Security Act) provides protection against assignment or alienation of benefits in qualified retirement plans. A QDRO is a statutory exception to this rule, allowing for the division of these assets pursuant to a domestic relations order without violating ERISA's anti-alienation provisions.

sections.title: QDRO vs. IRA Transfer Incident to Divorce

rows.label: Governing Law
values: ERISA, IRC Section 414(p)
IRC Section 408(d)(6)

rows.label: Plan Type
values: Qualified Retirement Plans (e.g., 401(k), Pension)
IRAs (Traditional, Roth, SEP, SIMPLE)

rows.label: Court Order Required
values: QDRO (Qualified Domestic Relations Order)
Divorce decree or separation agreement

rows.label: Taxable Event Trigger
values: No taxable event at the time of transfer to alternate payee
No taxable event at the time of transfer to former spouse

rows.label: Alternate Payee Options
values: Spouse, Former Spouse, Child, Dependent
Typically the former spouse

rows.label: Direct Transfer Needed
values: Yes, plan administrator must approve and execute
Yes, from one IRA trustee/custodian directly to another (or a new IRA)

title: Retirement Plan Selection and Integration
description: Recommend appropriate plan types for different situations
topics: Recommend appropriate plan types for different situations
Integrate retirement plans with overall financial plan

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Selecting the right retirement plan—and integrating it with other planning areas—is critical to maximizing benefits.

sections.title: Coordinating Multiple Income Sources
rows: Fixed or COLA, may have survivor benefit

sections.title: Social Security Integration
sections.content: Large Traditional accounts: Delay SS, do Roth conversions. Mostly Roth: May claim SS earlier. Have pension: May claim SS earlier. Married: Coordinate spousal strategies. Delaying SS from 62 to 70 = 77% increase in monthly benefit.

sections.title: Case Study: Comprehensive Integration
sections.content: Client: Both 60, $1.5M Traditional IRAs, $200K Roth, $300K taxable, $70K combined SS at 70, $100K spending. Strategy: Ages 60-63: Distributions from Traditional + Roth conversions (fill 22% bracket). Ages 64-69: Continue conversions, use taxable for living expenses. Age 70+: Start SS, take RMDs, use Roth for spikes. Result: Without strategy ~28% effective rate, with strategy ~18% effective rate.

sections.title: Summary
items: Match plan type to business structure, owner age, and contribution goals
SECURE 2.0: Super catch-up at 60-63, RMD age 75, Roth 401(k) no RMD
Integrate retirement with tax planning, estate planning, risk management
Strategic withdrawal sequencing can significantly reduce lifetime taxes
Delay Social Security usually optimal; creates Roth conversion window

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following retirement plans would be MOST suitable for a self-employed individual with no employees and a high income who wants to maximize tax-deferred savings?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A Solo 401(k) allows both employee and employer contributions, enabling a self-employed individual to contribute significantly more than a SEP IRA or SIMPLE IRA. While a Roth IRA offers tax-free growth, it has contribution limits that are much lower than a Solo 401(k).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the contribution limits and the individual's role as both employee and employer.

reveal.question: What is the new age for Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) under SECURE 2.0 for individuals who turn 72 after December 31, 2022, and turn 73 before January 1, 2033?
reveal.answer: RMDs are delayed until age 73. For those turning 74 after December 31, 2032, RMDs are further delayed to age 75.

rows.label: Investment Risk
values: Employer bears the risk
Employee bears the risk

rows.label: Benefit Amount
values: Predetermined based on formula (e.g., salary, years of service)
Depends on contributions and investment performance

rows.label: Contribution Limits
values: Actuarially determined, can be complex
Defined by IRS regulations (e.g., dollar limits, percentage of salary)

rows.label: Portability
values: Generally less portable; benefit may be frozen upon leaving employment
Generally more portable; account balance can be rolled over
